fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build a small lookup service for stdlib backport packages — basically a catalog that devs can query to figure out whether a given module has a backport they can install on older Python versions. Think of it like that compatibility table we maintain manually in the packaging tool, but this time it should be a proper service with a query interface so we stop duplicating that knowledge everywhere.

The service needs to handle a handful of operations: listing what's in the catalog, checking membership, rendering a nice human-readable output (with alignment, similar to how we did the indented listing in the dependency reporter), stripping version pins off identifiers, collapsing a bunch of identifiers down to their bare names, and expanding a bare name into all its versioned variants.

For the availability info, there's a special display convention for cases where a module never became native on a given Python line — just refer to the marker format we agreed on in the version formatting spec, you'll know it when you see it. The render output needs to be padded so all the separators line up nicely.

The expand operation should show the unversioned entry first, then pins newest-to-oldest. The adapter reads JSON from stdin and writes plain-text results to stdout. Any bad input should surface as a neutral error line, never a stack trace. One test runner script should exercise all the case files.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for the availability display, if something never became native on a major line, we should render that line with the exclamation marker format directly, so `!2` for the 2.x line and `!3` for the 3.x line. If it is known, render it as `major.minor` like `3.6`. Same rule everywhere we show versions, so in the render listing that means parentheses like `(!2, 3.3)`, and in expand that means the `versions=` line can look like `!2, 3.7`.

Also, on the pretty render output, the identifier column should be left-padded to a shared width based on the longest identifier currently in the catalog, so all the ` - ` separators line up cleanly. That width is computed from the catalog data at runtime, not hard-coded. And the render view should keep the catalog’s declaration order exactly as defined, not alphabetical order, with version-pinned variants staying next to their bare identifier the way they appear in the catalog. That’s different from `list_identifiers`, which should emit every distinct catalog identifier in ascending lexical order, with both bare identifiers and version-pinned variants as separate lines, and no duplicates.

For expand, the ordering is the bare entry first and then version-pinned variants newest-to-oldest. The output format should start with `entries=<count>`, then for each result a 1-based header `[<n>]`, an `identifier=<name>` line, one `url=<link>` line per distribution URL, and then `versions=<line2, line3>` using the same version rendering convention as above. And for the render operation in feature3_render_listing.json, each line is prefixed with `indent` spaces, then the identifier padded to the longest catalog identifier width, then ` - `, then the distribution URL, then the availability range in parentheses like `(2.3, 3.1)` or `(!2, 3.3)`.

trigger_keywords: how to separate bare name from version
what delimiter splits identifier from version
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A version pin is a trailing `==<version>` suffix. The bare name is everything before the first `==`. The identifier portion may contain word characters, underscores, and dots. A string with no `==` is returned unchanged. The strip operation echoes `input=<original>` then `identifier=<bare>`.

The unversioned operation strips version pins from all input identifiers, de-duplicates them, and emits `identifier=<name>` lines in ascending lexical order. Multiple pinned variants sharing a bare name collapse to exactly one output line, and a bare name already present collapses together with its pins.
Any failure or unrecognized input must be rendered as a neutral `error=<category>` line (e.g. `error=unknown_op`). It must never produce a host-language runtime exception or stack trace on stdout.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness work
output file naming convention for tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test runner is invoked as `bash rcb_tests/test.sh` and accepts `--cases-dir <subdir>` (default `test_cases`). For each case it writes a file to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt` containing only raw stdout. For example, the first case in `feature1_list_identifiers.json` run with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` goes to `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_list_identifiers@000.txt`. Outputs are namespaced by cases-dir so different runs never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: what fields does a catalog entry have
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each catalog entry has three parts: an identifier (bare or version-pinned with `==<version>` suffix), one or more distribution URLs, and an availability range as a pair `(line2_min, line3_min)` — the earliest 2.x and 3.x versions where the module is natively present (i.e., the backport is no longer needed). Bare and pinned identifiers are independent entries that may carry different URLs and ranges.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'list_identifiers', 'is_backport', 'render', 'strip_version', 'unversioned', 'expand'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the marker format we agreed on in the version formatting spec
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The exclamation-prefixed line marker convention: `!2` when functionality never became natively available on the Python 2.x line, `!3` for the 3.x line; used in both the render listing parentheses and the expand `versions=` lines.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that compatibility table we maintain manually in the packaging tool
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The hard-coded backport catalog data (identifiers, distribution URLs, availability ranges) that must be decoupled from query/formatting logic — as defined by the entries visible in the expected outputs of the test cases, e.g. argparse, asyncio, configparser, contextvars, dataclasses, enum, faulthandler, importlib, ipaddress, mock, statistics, typing, typing_extensions and its pinned variants.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. Please refer to the existing test case files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the exact expected input/output contracts, and look at the PRD's Core Features section for behavioral descriptions. If something still seems ambiguous, implement the simplest interpretation that satisfies all provided test cases.

feature1_list_identifiers.json.description: Enumerate every distinct catalog identifier (module entry, including version-pinned variants) known to the backport catalog. Output lists one identifier per line in ascending lexical order.

feature2_membership_check.json.description: Report whether a given identifier is a member of the catalog. Echoes the queried identifier and a boolean membership flag. Version-pinned variants are members; arbitrary standard-library or third-party names are not.

feature3_render_listing.json.description: Render the full catalog as an aligned human-readable listing indented by a caller-supplied number of leading spaces. Each line shows the identifier, the distribution URL, and the (py2, py3) availability range in parentheses; a missing major-line bound is shown as an exclamation-prefixed marker.

cases.expected_output:
argparse               - https://pypi.org/project/argparse/ (2.3, 3.1)

   configparser           - https://pypi.org/project/configparser/ (2.6, 3.0)

   dataclasses            - https://pypi.org/project/dataclasses/ (!2, 3.6)

   faulthandler           - https://pypi.org/project/faulthandler/ (2.6, 3.0)
   importlib              - https://pypi.org/project/importlib/ (2.3, 3.0)
   ipaddress              - https://pypi.org/project/ipaddress/ (2.6, 3.2)
   mock                   - https://pypi.org/project/mock/ (!2, 3.6)

feature4_strip_version.json.description: Strip a trailing version-pin suffix (an identifier followed by == and any text) and return the bare identifier. Names containing word characters, underscores, and dots are preserved; a name without a version pin is returned unchanged.

feature5_unversioned_set.json.description: Collapse a collection of identifiers into the set of bare identifiers by stripping every version pin and de-duplicating. Output lists the resulting identifiers one per line in ascending lexical order; version-pinned variants of the same base collapse to a single entry.

feature6_expand_versions.json.description: Expand a bare identifier into all its catalog entries (the unversioned entry plus every version-pinned variant), ordered with the unversioned entry first and version-pinned variants from newest to oldest. Each entry lists its identifier, distribution URL(s), and (py2, py3) availability range.
